query: effect of metformin on vitamin B12 levels

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 deficiency can occasionally arise with long-term metformin use. Consequently, the ADA advises frequent monitoring of vitamin B12 levels, especially in patients with anemia or peripheral neuropathy. Moreover, patients on concomitant drugs, which can cause an increased risk of lactic acidosis, should undergo regular monitoring. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Less commonly, some patients may experience chest discomfort, headache, diaphoresis, hypoglycemia, weakness, and rhinitis while taking metformin. Prolonged use of metformin has been associated with decreased vitamin B12 levels; therefore, healthcare professionals should carefully monitor patients, especially those with anemia or peripheral neuropathy. In some cases, supplementation of vitamin B12 may be necessary. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Hypoglycemic Medications -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: In patients taking metformin, initial and frequent monitoring of hemoglobin, RBC indices, and renal function tests before therapy initiation, and at least every year. The clinician should repeat these tests every 3 to 6 months if the glomerular filtration rate is 45 to less than 60 mL/min/1.73 m2, and every three months if the glomerular filtration rate is from 30 to less than 45 mL/min/1.73 m^2. Serum vitamin B12 and folate levels should be measured if the patient is on long-term metformin to rule out megaloblastic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin) -- Indications -- FDA approved indications:
retrieved_docs.content: Long-term metformin use: May correlate with reduced intestinal mobility or intestinal bacterial overgrowth that competes with vitamin B12 and decreases its absorption.

retrieved_docs.title: Pernicious Anemia -- Differential Diagnosis -- Other causes of B12 deficiency:
retrieved_docs.content: food-cobalamin malabsorption veganism metformin long-term proton pump inhibitor therapy estrogen contraceptive pills gastric surgery ileal disease ileal resection

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Etiology -- Drug-Induced
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin: Metformin, a common anti-diabetic medication, can cause B12 deficiency in approximately 10% to 30% of patients. The mechanism involves interference with calcium-dependent ileal absorption of the B12-intrinsic factor complex. The effect of metformin on B12 absorption is reversible with calcium supplementation. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Toxin exposure: Exposure to nitrous oxide can cause vitamin B12 deficiency and neurologic symptoms. Metformin treatment can cause B12 deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Hypoglycemic Medications -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin : Gastrointestinal upset such as diarrhea (12% to 53%), nausea and vomiting (7% to 26%), flatulence (4% to 12%), chest discomfort, flushing, palpitation, headache (5% to 6%), chills, dizziness, taste disorder, diaphoresis, nail disease, skin rash, vitamin B12 deficiency. Also, in less than 1% of patients, it causes lactic acidosis, which can be life-threatening, and is precipitated by conditions predisposing to hypoperfusion and hypoxemia, such as severe renal failure (eGFR less than 30 ml/min/1.73 m2).

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pre-Diabetes and diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: For type 2 diabetes, metformin is always the first-line treatment unless it is contraindicated or not tolerated. Metformin is cheap, has a low risk of hypoglycemia, has proven mortality benefits in overweight individuals, and has potential mortality benefits in all patients with type 2 diabetes. [14] It should be started at a lower dose of 500 mg daily or twice daily and titrated up. The most common adverse effect is diarrhea or gastrointestinal upset. Other important adverse effects are lactic acidosis in those with reduced GFR <30 ml/min/1.73m^2, vitamin B12 deficiency, and worsening neuropathy with prolonged use. Metformin is contraindicated with eGFR <30 ml/min/1.73m^2, unstable heart failure, liver disease, active alcohol abuse, and history of lactic acidosis during metformin therapy. Metformin should not be initiated in those with eGFR < 45 ml/min/1.73m^2. However, in patients already on metformin, it can be continued until eGFR is <30.

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanocobalamin -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Long-term metformin use [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Nephropathy -- Treatment / Management -- Metformin and Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists
retrieved_docs.content: The KDIGO guidelines and the American Diabetes Association (ADA) suggest using metformin alongside dietary modifications as first-line treatment for T2D patients with CKD and an eGFR greater than 30 mL/min/1.73m 2 . Metformin has demonstrated significant benefits in CKD progression, cardiovascular outcomes, and all-cause mortality. However, metformin should not be initiated in individuals with an eGFR of less than 45 mL/min/1.73m 2 , as they are at risk of progressing to an eGFR of less than 30 mL/min/1.73m 2 and developing lactic acidosis. The metformin dosage should be halved for eGFR between 45 and 60 mL/min/1.73m 2 . Metformin should also be withheld during inpatient admissions to prevent complications from potential renal insults. Additionally, metformin may reduce vitamin B12 and folate levels, necessitating regular monitoring and supplementation as needed. [2] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 deficiency is a serious disorder that, if not treated, can lead to severe neurological symptoms. The ideal way to manage the disorder is with an interprofessional team that includes a primary clinician, gastroenterologist, neurologist, surgeon, pharmacist, dietitian, and a nurse. The primary focus today is to try to prevent the disorder in the first place. The nurse, dietitian, and pharmacist can help educate the patient that there is an increased risk among family members who should be screened for the disorder. Further, any patient who has undergone gastric resection surgery is also at risk for vitamin B12 deficiency and should be regularly tested. The pharmacist should also recommend testing in patients treated with metformin and proton pump inhibitors. Finally, vitamin B12 deficiency is common in seniors because of poor nutrition, dementia, rigid vegetarian diet, or lack of access to care. These individuals should be proactively screened for vitamin B12 deficiency. Once treated, the individuals should be followed by a visiting home care nurse to make sure that the neurological symptoms are improving. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocytosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In most cases, the etiology of macrocytosis may be related to abnormal RBC development, abnormal RBC membrane composition, increased reticulocyte count, or a combination of these 3 factors. Abnormal RBC development may occur in settings such as vitamin B12 or folate deficiency. Vitamin B12 deficiency may be related to prolonged, strictly vegan diets. B12 is dependent on normal gastric and small intestine function. Its absorption needs intrinsic factors from the stomach and normal small bowel absorption. Immune interference with the intrinsic factor results in no B12 absorption. Impaired vitamin B12 absorption may occur due to gastrointestinal abnormalities, including pernicious anemia, gastritis, celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), as a late complication of gastrointestinal surgeries such as gastrectomy, gastric bypass, and ileal resection, as an adverse reaction to medications such as proton pump inhibitors and metformin, or as an adverse effect of nitrous oxide abuse. Folate deficiency may be dietary, especially in cases involving alcoholic or elderly patients. Folic acid absorption also depends on health good GI small bowel absorption.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Screening for vitamin B12 deficiency should be considered in patients receiving chronic treatment with proton pump inhibitors, H2 antagonists, and metformin. Patients with atrophic gastritis, gastrectomy, and pancreatectomy may also benefit from routine screening. In addition, preoperative screening for cobalamin deficiency should be considered for patients requiring anesthesia with nitrous oxide.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Etiology -- Drug-Induced
retrieved_docs.content: Several medications, such as gastric acid suppressants, metformin, and nitrous oxide, can result in vitamin B12 deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Certain medications, such as metformin, proton pump inhibitors, and nitrous oxide, have been associated with vitamin B12 deficiency. Proton pump inhibitors reduce gastric acid production, impairing vitamin B12 dissociation from its protein-bound form in the diet. Metformin is the best-known medication causing B12 deficiency by interfering with calcium-dependent ileal absorption of B12. The effect can be reversed with calcium supplements. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as agents containing vitamin B12 and folic acid, and they are specifically described as folic‑acid/vitamin B12 formulations. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is likewise placed within the broader category of vitamin B12 and folic‑acid compounds. Within this vitamin B12 group, individual forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin are recognized as distinct members. Clinical assessment often includes laboratory measures that quantify both folic acid and cyanocobalamin (the active form of vitamin B12) in serum or whole‑blood specimens, providing a nominal‑type impression of the patient’s nutritional status. Together, these relationships clarify that antianemic therapy commonly relies on combined vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplementation, that various biochemical forms of vitamin B12 (including mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin) are part of this therapeutic class, and that combined measurement of folic acid and cyanocobalamin is a key tool for monitoring treatment effectiveness and guiding patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
retrieved_docs.content: B₁₂ family, mecobalamin and hydroxocobalamin are members of “Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues),” reflecting alternative cobalamin analogues used in supplementation and therapy. Overall, the taxonomy connects specific vitamin forms, iron compounds, and combined supplement products to the categories of antianemic therapy, iron supplementation, and the prevention or treatment of nutritional deficiencies. This structured framework enables clinicians to select formulations that simultaneously address iron deficiency, vitamin‑dependent metabolic needs, and broader micronutrient gaps, particularly in populations with increased iron requirements, malabsorption risk, or postoperative bariatric status.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2242
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is a specific form of vitamin B12 that is commonly grouped with folic acid in clinical contexts, reflecting the practice of assessing both nutrients together. Mecobalamin, also known as methylcobalamin, is a distinct methylated derivative of vitamin B12 and is considered a member of the broader vitamin B12 family. Laboratory testing that measures vitamin B12 and folic acid often uses a combined assay described as “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型,” which quantifies cyanocobalamin (a vitamin B12 analogue) and folic acid (叶酸) in serum or whole‑blood specimens and reports the results as nominal values. This integrated measurement approach aids clinicians in evaluating a patient’s B12 and folate status simultaneously, supporting more comprehensive nutritional assessment and guiding appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic acid formulations, and they are also described as folic acid / vitamin B12 preparations. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is likewise categorized under vitamin B12 and folic acid, indicating that this group of compounds belongs to the same nutrient class used to treat anemia. Within the Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) group, mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin are identified as specific members, representing distinct chemical analogues of vitamin B12. Together, these relationships show that antianemic preparations and the various vitamin B12 analogues share a common therapeutic focus on supplying vitamin B12 and folic acid, with mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin being particular forms employed in such treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1283

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1521
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as formulations that contain vitamin B12 and folic acid, and they are specifically described as folic acid / vitamin B12 preparations. Clinical assessment of the effectiveness and adequacy of these anti‑anemic agents involves measuring vitamin B12 and folic acid levels, which are reported as “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型” – a laboratory notation indicating the impression, time point, and specimen type (serum or whole blood) for the nominal values of folic acid (叶酸) and cyanocobalamin (氰钴胺素, vitamin B12). This integrated understanding links the composition of anti‑anemic preparations with the standardized laboratory measures used to monitor patient response and guide therapeutic decisions.
